urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.drafted_comment: The shift from "code to execute" to "prompt to orchestrate" is interesting, but I think we're underestimating how much deeper understanding still matters. When your BEV transformer hallucinates phantom objects or your agentic system gets stuck in degenerate loops, you can't prompt your way out without knowing why the failure modes exist. The real question is whether prompt engineering becomes a new kind of systems thinking or just a brittle interface layer that breaks under distribution shift.
urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Data Science & Python - Scientist, Analyst
urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.author: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Data Science & Python - Scientist, Analyst
urn:li:activity:7475118134287556608.text:
First we learned to code computers. Now we’re learning to prompt them 🤷🏽‍♂️

New things that AI Introduces:

👉 Prompt Engineering: Talking to AI in the right way to get better results.

👉 Vibe Coding: Building software by guiding AI instead of writing every line yourself.

👉 AI-Assisted Developer: A programmer who collaborates with AI to code faster.

👉 Human-in-the-Loop: AI works, humans review and approve.

👉 Hallucination: When AI confidently invents wrong information.

👉 Copilot Workflow: Using AI as a real-time assistant for work and learning.

👉 Context Window: How much information AI can remember during a conversation.

👉 Agentic AI: AI that can plan, reason, and perform tasks autonomously.

👉 Synthetic Content: AI-generated text, images, audio, video, or code.

And finally : Understanding: An optional feature in modern programming. 😄

urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.drafted_comment: I see the analogy, but calling MCP a "nervous system" might overstate its current adoption. Most production agentic systems I've seen still rely on custom tool integration layers rather than a universal protocol. Are you seeing MCP deployed widely enough to be considered infrastructure, or is this more aspirational? Also curious how you'd fit multimodal perception into this body framework, since modern agents increasingly need vision and sensor fusion beyond just language understanding.
urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence 🟥
urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.author: Artificial Intelligence 🟥
urn:li:activity:7475118183381729281.text:
Nobody explained AI this clearly until now.
Stop memorizing definitions.

Start thinking in analogies. 🧠
Here's the human body framework that finally makes AI systems click 👇

1️⃣ LLM = Brain

The reasoning engine.

Learns patterns, understands language, generates intelligent responses.

Without it? Nothing thinks.
2️⃣ RAG = Brain + Books

The brain that can also read.

Retrieves relevant information from external sources at query time — producing accurate, up-to-date answers.

Without it? The brain guesses.
3️⃣ AI Agent = Brain + Hands

The brain that can also act.

Plans, decides, uses tools, APIs, and external systems to complete real-world tasks autonomously — start to finish.

Without it? The brain only talks.
4️⃣ MCP = Nervous System

The universal wiring.

Standardizes how the AI brain communicates with tools, data, and external services — so any model connects to any system through one consistent protocol.

Without it? Nothing coordinates.

The complete picture:
🧠 Thinks → 📚 Knows → 🤖 Acts → ⚡ Wires It All
LLM → RAG → Agent → MCP
This is not four separate technologies.

This is one intelligent system — built layer by layer.

Most people in 2026 are still using just Layer 1.
The professionals pulling ahead have all four working together.
Which layer are you currently building on? 👇

♻️ Share this with someone who finds AI confusing. This analogy changes everything.
.
.
.
.
.

urn:li:activity:7475118190327406592.drafted_comment: I'm curious how you think about the gap between assuming a parametric distribution and what actually happens in high-dimensional spaces. Most real-world data I've worked with (especially in perception tasks) doesn't cleanly follow any of these classical forms, and forcing a Gaussian prior can sometimes hurt more than help. Do you find mixture models or non-parametric approaches like kernel density estimation more useful when the underlying structure is genuinely unknown, or is that overthinking it for most applied work?

urn:li:activity:7475118190327406592.text:
📊 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗦𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 & 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴

One of the most important concepts every Data Scientist, Data Analyst, and Machine Learning Engineer should understand is Probability Distributions.

Whether you're building predictive models, performing statistical analysis, detecting anomalies, or evaluating uncertainty, probability distributions help describe how data behaves in the real world.

The image below highlights some of the most widely used distributions in statistics and data science.

🔹 𝗡𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Often used to model natural phenomena and measurement errors.

🔹 𝗕𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗹𝗶 & 𝗕𝗶𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Ideal for modeling binary outcomes such as Success/Failure, Yes/No, or Click/No Click.

🔹 𝗣𝗼𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Commonly used to model the number of events occurring within a fixed interval of time or space.

🔹 𝗣𝗼𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Helps estimate waiting times between events.

🔹 𝗨𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Assumes all outcomes have equal probability.

🔹 𝗚𝗮𝗺𝗺𝗮 & 𝗪𝗲𝗶𝗯𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Frequently applied in reliability engineering, survival analysis, and risk modeling.

🔹 𝗖𝗵𝗶-𝗦𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗲, 𝘁-𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗙-𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Essential for hypothesis testing and statistical inference.

🔹 𝗟𝗼𝗴-𝗡𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Commonly found in financial data, income distributions, and business metrics.

🔹 𝗕𝗲𝘁𝗮 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 – Widely used in Bayesian statistics and probability estimation.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗗𝗼𝗲𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿?

✅ Better understanding of data behavior
✅ More accurate model selection
✅ Improved feature engineering
✅ Stronger statistical reasoning
✅ Better interpretation of machine learning results

Many professionals focus directly on algorithms, but the strongest data practitioners understand the statistical foundations behind the data first.

Data Science is not just about building models—it's about understanding uncertainty, variability, and patterns hidden within data.

What probability distribution do you encounter most often in your projects?

🚀 Looking to build a career in Data Analytics, Data Science, Machine Learning, or AI?

Success isn't about learning everything—it's about learning the right skills in the right order.

Platforms like 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗙𝗹𝗮𝗶𝗿 help learners with:

✅ Structured learning paths from Python to Generative AI
✅ Hands-on projects for real-world experience
✅ Practical case studies and problem-solving skills
✅ Coding practice and interview preparation
✅ Industry-relevant, regularly updated content

💡 In the fast-changing world of Data & AI, continuous learning and practical application are the keys to staying ahead.

Invest in skills today to create opportunities tomorrow. 🚀

𝐑𝐀𝐆 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐩𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐧 𝐀𝐈 𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐛𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐡𝐨𝐥𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐬.

Over the last year, one challenge has surfaced repeatedly across enterprise AI initiatives: strong pilot results often fail to translate into production trust. The issue is rarely the model itself. More often, it is the quality of retrieval, grounding, and evaluation behind the responses.

What stands out in the most successful deployments is a shift in focus from model performance to information reliability.

1. Prioritized retrieval quality as a core design principle rather than a downstream optimization exercise.

2. Established evaluation frameworks around relevance, grounding, and answer accuracy instead of relying solely on user feedback.

3. Focused on context enrichment strategies to improve response quality in domain-specific environments.

4. Recognized that hallucinations are often symptoms of poor information architecture, not just model limitations.

5. Evaluated production scalability early, avoiding costly redesigns when moving beyond proof-of-concept environments.

Enterprise AI success is not determined by how well a model generates answers. It is determined by how confidently the business can trust those answers.

The organizations seeing meaningful value from RAG are treating retrieval, governance, and evaluation as strategic capabilities rather than technical implementation details.

This cookbook offers a useful framework for teams focused on moving AI initiatives from pilot-stage experimentation to production-scale outcomes.
: https://lnkd.in/dF3xgWeq

urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.drafted_comment: This reminds me of the "no free lunch" theorem in ML, but applied to deployment strategy. I've seen teams at IIT Roorkee benchmark new architectures against ImageNet-pretrained ResNets and assume the delta justifies production costs, but they rarely factor in inference latency or model drift over time. For something like credit propensity, do you find that explainability constraints (SHAP values, feature importance) actually push teams back toward simpler baselines more often than the raw performance numbers would suggest?
urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated)
urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.author: AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated)
urn:li:activity:7475118295424098304.text:
The cubic cat has more value than it may seem. In physics, a simplification can provide a reasonable first answer. In data science, the equivalent is building a baseline before investing in more complex models.

That baseline can be a simple rule, a historical average, or a basic statistical model. What matters is that it works as a point of comparison.

The business question is direct: does the improvement from the advanced model justify the additional cost?

In banking, a credit propensity model should first be compared against simple rules: income, payment history, average balance, and recent behavior.

In marketing, a conversion model should be measured against a basic segmentation of active, inactive, frequent, and high-value customers.

In planning, an advanced forecast should outperform a reference built with historical sales, seasonality, and known commercial events.

Without that comparison, it is easy to confuse sophistication with impact.

With the baseline, the conversation becomes clearer: how much it improves, how much it costs, how long it takes, and how explainable it is for the business.

Building the cubic cat first helps us decide when it is worth building the real cat.

What business decision in your team would improve if it had a clear baseline first?


Follow 👉 Daniel Zaldaña to explore more about AI and data science.


#AI
#ArtificialIntelligence
#DataScience

urn:li:activity:7475118340538019844.drafted_comment: I've found that in perception tasks, especially with point clouds, classical methods like DBSCAN still outperform deep learning when you have geometric priors and limited labeled data. The semi-supervised section mentions self-training, but there's growing work on contrastive pretraining (like PointContrast) that bridges unsupervised representation learning with downstream fine-tuning more effectively. Have you seen cases where the classical taxonomy here breaks down, especially when dealing with multi-modal sensor fusion where supervision exists at different levels across modalities?

urn:li:activity:7475118340538019844.author: 🦾 Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Deep Learning, Computer Vision, Robotics, Gen AI, GPT
urn:li:activity:7475118340538019844.text:
📊 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱

Machine Learning continues to be one of the most impactful technologies driving innovation across industries. While new techniques emerge rapidly, mastering the fundamentals remains essential for building reliable and scalable AI solutions.

🔹 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Used when training data contains labeled outcomes.

𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Logistic Regression
• Decision Trees
• Random Forest
• Support Vector Machines (SVM)
• K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
• Naive Bayes

𝗥𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Linear Regression
• Multiple Regression
• Lasso Regression

🔹 𝗨𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Used to identify hidden patterns in unlabeled data.

𝗖𝗹𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴
• K-Means
• DBSCAN

𝗗𝗶𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗥𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
• Independent Component Analysis (ICA)

𝗔𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗹𝘆 𝗗𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Isolation Forest
• Z-Score Methods

🔹 𝗦𝗲𝗺𝗶-𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Combines a small amount of labeled data with a large volume of unlabeled data.

• Self-Training
• Co-Training

🔹 𝗥𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Enables systems to learn through rewards and interactions.

• Q-Learning
• Policy Optimization
• Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

💡 The most successful ML practitioners are not those who know the most algorithms, but those who understand when, why, and how to apply them effectively.

Which algorithm has had the biggest impact on your projects and learning journey?

urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.drafted_comment: This framing assumes clean data is even achievable at scale, but in practice most interesting ML problems involve learning useful representations *despite* noisy labels and distribution shifts. The ImageNet dataset that powered the deep learning revolution had a 6% error rate, and foundation models today are trained on scraped web data full of inconsistencies. I wonder if the real skill isn't perfect data curation but designing architectures and training procedures robust enough to extract signal from messy, real-world sources.
urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Python, Data Science, Agentic AI Scientist
urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.author: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Python, Data Science, Agentic AI Scientist
urn:li:activity:7475117641855139841.text:
𝑨𝑰 𝑾𝒐𝒏'𝒕 𝑭𝒊𝒙 𝑩𝒂𝒅 𝑫𝒂𝒕𝒂!

You can build the smartest, most advanced models in the world—but if your foundation is weak, everything on top will eventually collapse. 💥

Too many teams get caught up in the hype:

👉 "AI First" sounds exciting.

👉 "Data First" is what actually works.


Before you start chasing complex algorithms, you need to ruthlessly focus on:

🔹 Data Quality – Garbage in, garbage out.
🔹 Clear Ownership – Who is responsible for what?
🔹 Strong Governance – Setting the right rules.
🔹 Reliable Metrics – Measuring what actually matters.
🔹 Trust – Can you actually rely on your data?

The bottom line: AI is only as good as the data it learns from. Build the foundation right. Then scale.

urn:li:activity:7475117795819835392.drafted_comment: The water usage angle is important, but I think the framing misses a key technical opportunity. A lot of recent work on model distillation and quantization (like the BitNet papers from Microsoft) shows we can maintain strong performance while drastically cutting inference costs. The real question isn't just "AI uses too many resources" but why we're still deploying 175B parameter models for tasks that 7B fine-tuned versions handle just as well. Are we optimizing for benchmark leaderboards or actual deployment efficiency?

urn:li:activity:7475117795819835392.author: 🦾 Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Deep Learning, Computer Vision, Robotics, Gen AI, GPT
urn:li:activity:7475117795819835392.text:
This isn't a fun fact—
it's the hidden cost of AI you never see.

Every prompt feels weightless.
But behind the screen, thousands of servers 
generate massive amounts of heat 24/7.

Keeping those systems cool requires 
enormous amounts of water and energy.

We often talk about model performance.

We rarely talk about the resources required 
to run those models at scale.

🧠 Understand the real resource 
intensity of the models we build.

The future of AI isn't just about intelligence.
It's also about efficiency and sustainability.

Follow Harshit Harsh for simple Data Science & AI insights.
Repost or save this if it changed how you think about AI.
#ArtificialIntelligence #DataScience #AIInfrastructure #DataCenters #MachineLearning #Sustainability #Technology

urn:li:activity:7475127451824713728.drafted_comment: I'm curious why semi-supervised learning is still framed as a separate paradigm when techniques like self-training are really just iterative supervised learning with pseudo-labeling. In most production settings I've seen, people either go fully supervised with active learning for labeling efficiency or leverage pretrained representations, which doesn't fit neatly into this taxonomy. Have you encountered scenarios where classic semi-supervised methods like co-training actually outperformed these alternatives on real datasets?

urn:li:activity:7475127451824713728.author: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Python, Data Science, Agentic AI Scientist
urn:li:activity:7475127451824713728.text:
🚀 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗦𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘀𝘁 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄

Machine Learning is not just about building models—it's about selecting the right algorithm for the right problem. A strong understanding of core machine learning algorithms helps data professionals make better decisions, improve model performance, and solve real-world business challenges effectively.

🔹 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Used when historical labeled data is available.

✅ 𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀:
• Logistic Regression
• Decision Trees
• Random Forest
• Support Vector Machine (SVM)
• K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
• Naive Bayes

✅ 𝗥𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀:
• Linear Regression
• Multiple Regression
• Lasso Regression

🔹 𝗨𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Used to discover hidden patterns in unlabeled data.

✅ 𝗖𝗹𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴:
• K-Means Clustering
• DBSCAN

✅ 𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗰𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 & 𝗣𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴:
• Apriori Algorithm
• Frequent Pattern Growth (FP-Growth)

✅ 𝗗𝗶𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗥𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻:
• Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
• Independent Component Analysis (ICA)

✅ 𝗔𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗹𝘆 𝗗𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻:
• Z-Score Method
• Isolation Forest

🔹 𝗦𝗲𝗺𝗶-𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Combines small amounts of labeled data with large amounts of unlabeled data.

• Self-Training
• Co-Training

🔹 𝗥𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Enables agents to learn through rewards and penalties.

• Q-Learning
• Policy Optimization
• Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

💡 Learning algorithms is important, but understanding when to use them, their assumptions, strengths, limitations, and evaluation metrics is what truly differentiates a Machine Learning practitioner.

The journey from data to intelligent decision-making starts with mastering these fundamentals.

Which Machine Learning algorithm do you use most frequently in your projects? Share your experience in the comments. 👇

🚀 Looking to build a strong foundation in 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝗜?

Machine Learning is not about memorizing algorithms—it's about understanding core concepts and applying them to solve real-world problems.

Platforms like 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗙𝗹𝗮𝗶𝗿 help learners with:

✅ Structured learning paths
✅ Hands-on projects with real datasets
✅ Regression, Classification, Clustering & Deep Learning
✅ Model building and evaluation techniques
✅ Interview preparation and coding practice

💡 The key to mastering Machine Learning is simple: build projects, experiment with models, and learn by solving real problems.

📊 Every successful AI solution starts with strong ML fundamentals.

urn:li:activity:7475127493176365056.drafted_comment: The LiDAR sensing improvements are promising, but I'm curious how these L3 systems handle edge cases where perception degrades faster than the 10-second SAE J3016 takeover window allows. The Mercedes Drive Pilot approach of geofencing to mapped highways seems conservative, but given the liability shift at L3, are we seeing OEMs prioritize smaller operational design domains over broader capability to manage legal risk rather than technical limitations?

urn:li:activity:7475127493176365056.text:
𝐋𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥 𝟑 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐧𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐮𝐬 𝐕𝐞𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐥𝐞 (𝐀𝐃𝐀𝐒) 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐤𝐞𝐭:- 𝐀𝐧 𝐔𝐥𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐏𝐃𝐅 𝐆𝐮𝐢𝐝𝐞!

𝐃𝐨𝐰𝐧𝐥𝐨𝐚𝐝 𝐏𝐃𝐅: https://lnkd.in/dHqFdQAT

Level 3 autonomy is a crucial step toward true autonomous vehicles as it requires balancing regulatory norms and advanced sensing technologies for reliable advanced features. Industry experts say the sensing, computing, and software technologies available today can achieve autonomous driving.

The level 3 autonomous vehicle market is projected to grow from 291 thousand units in 2025 to 8.7 million units by 2035 at a CAGR of 40.5%.

Driver: Need for improving sensing technologies aimed toward level 3 autonomy

Sensor technology has advanced rapidly in recent years, laying the groundwork for scalable level 3 autonomy. For level 3 autonomous vehicles, LiDAR systems have significantly improved cost efficiency and resolution. For instance, Valeo’s latest SCALA 3 unit delivers up to 12 million points per second. It can detect objects at ranges of up to 300 meters—enabling high-speed, “eyes-off” functionality in traffic jam scenarios at speeds up to 130 km/h.

Opportunity: Strong potential from subscription packages

OEMs increasingly monetize ADAS/automated driving through software subscriptions and feature unlocks. Since Level 3 systems require powerful computing and up-to-date mapping/software, vendors see recurring fees as viable. Surveys also support this. For example, AlixPartners found that ~60% of consumers are open to subscribing to ADAS features. In practice, Mercedes-Benz became the first to offer a certified level 3 system (Drive Pilot) as a paid service.

Global Level 3 Autonomous Vehicle Market Ecosystem Analysis

The market ecosystem participants include OEMs, tier 1 suppliers, autonomous vehicle developers, software and system providers, chip makers, and sensor providers. A few major level 3 autonomous vehicle providers include #RobertBoschGmbH (Germany), #Continental AG (Germany), #DensoCorporation (Japan), #ZFFriedrichshafen AG (Germany), and #MagnaInternational Inc. (Canada).

𝐆𝐞𝐭 𝐌𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐃𝐞𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐥𝐬: https://lnkd.in/dHqFdQAT

𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐏𝐥𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫𝐬: 

The Level 3 Autonomous Vehicle Market is dominated by a few major players that have a wide regional presence. The major players in the Level 3 Autonomous Vehicle Market are Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Honda.

urn:li:activity:7475127496255090688.drafted_comment: I'm curious about the operational gap between layers 5 and 6. Most current "agentic" systems still rely heavily on LLM-based planning with brittle tool use, which makes them closer to sophisticated prompt chains than true agents with state representation and robust action spaces. Do you see hybrid architectures like ReAct or Reflexion as temporary scaffolding, or do you think the agent layer fundamentally requires different inductive biases than what transformers provide?

urn:li:activity:7475127496255090688.author: 🦾 Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Deep Learning, Computer Vision, Robotics, Gen AI, GPT
urn:li:activity:7475127496255090688.text:
🚀 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟳 𝗟𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗜 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗝𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗥𝘂𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲

Artificial Intelligence is often discussed as a single technology, but in reality, it's a stack of evolving capabilities built layer by layer. Understanding these layers helps us see where today's AI stands and where the future may lead.

1️⃣ 𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗜

📌 The foundation of AI.

•  Built on predefined rules and decision trees.
•  Every action follows logic programmed by humans.
•  Effective for structured and predictable tasks.

2️⃣ 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴

📌 AI starts learning from data.

•  Systems identify patterns instead of relying only on rules.
•  Improves performance through experience.
•  Powers recommendations, fraud detection, and forecasting.

3️⃣ 𝗡𝗲𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗡𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀

📌 Inspired by the human brain.

•  Uses interconnected layers of artificial neurons.
•  Processes large volumes of complex information.
•  Forms the backbone of many modern AI systems.

4️⃣ 𝗗𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴

📌 Neural networks at scale.

•  Handles images, speech, text, and video.
•  Enables breakthroughs in computer vision and natural language processing.
•  Responsible for many recent AI advancements.

5️⃣ 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗔𝗜

📌 AI that creates.

•  Generates text, images, code, audio, and videos.
•  Helps automate content creation and problem-solving.
•  Examples include modern AI assistants and creative tools.

6️⃣ 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗔𝗜

📌 AI that acts.

•  Goes beyond generating outputs.
•  Can plan, reason, use tools, and execute tasks.
•  Represents the next major shift toward autonomous workflows.

7️⃣ 𝗔𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 (𝗔𝗚𝗜)

📌 The future vision.

•  Human-level intelligence across diverse domains.
•  Able to learn, reason, and adapt to new situations independently.
•  Still a research goal rather than a reality.

𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝗪𝗲 𝗧𝗼𝗱𝗮𝘆?

We are moving from the era of Generative AI to Agentic AI, where systems can not only create content but also plan, act, and complete tasks autonomously.

Organizations that understand these AI layers today will be better positioned to capitalize on tomorrow’s opportunities.

💡 Which do you think will have the greatest business impact over the next five years: Generative AI, Agentic AI, or AGI?

💬 Want to explore 𝗔𝗜, 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗮, and emerging tech trends? 𝗗𝗠 me your thoughts.

urn:li:activity:7475127500356874240.drafted_comment: This reminds me of the diagnostic reasoning work in Bayesian networks, where you need multiple independent evidence sources to actually narrow down the posterior probability of a failure mode. But I'm curious: how do you handle cases where the evidence itself is unreliable or incomplete (like missing logs during the exact failure window, or metrics that weren't instrumented yet)? Most RCA frameworks I've seen assume clean observability, but production systems rarely give you that luxury.
urn:li:activity:7475127500356874240.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Data Science & Python - Scientist, Analyst [7475127500356874240]
urn:li:activity:7475127500356874240.author: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Generative AI, Data Science & Python - Scientist, Analyst
urn:li:activity:7475127500356874240.text:
“Root Cause Analysis Should Not Start With a Guess.”

Most incident workflows still look like this:
Alert fires 🚨
Engineer opens dashboards 📊
Logs are searched manually 🧾
Recent deployments are checked 🔁
Kubernetes events are reviewed ☸️
Someone proposes a likely cause 🧠
👉 Everything seems “under investigation.”
⚠️ But here’s the real question:
👉 Is the root cause supported by evidence, or is it just the most plausible explanation?

🧠 What I See in Real Teams
A pod restarts… and the application is blamed
A deployment fails… and the latest commit is blamed
CPU usage spikes… and the workload is immediately right-sized
A Pending pod appears… and node capacity is assumed to be the problem
An OOMKilled event occurs… but current requests and limits are not checked
An image fails to pull… but the registry, tag and secret are not validated
👉 A likely explanation is not the same as a proven root cause.
👉 Production remediation should not be based on guesswork.

💡 The Hidden Risk
RCA does not fail only because:
👉 Engineers lack data
It fails because:
👉 The evidence is fragmented across too many systems
Metrics show symptoms.
Logs show application behavior.
Events show scheduler and kubelet decisions.
Deployments show desired configuration.
Git shows recent changes.
GitOps shows declared intent.
Runtime state shows what is actually happening.
Looking at only one signal creates an incomplete diagnosis.
And an incomplete diagnosis can lead to:
The wrong Kubernetes patch.
Unnecessary scaling.
A risky rollback.
More cloud cost.
A repeated incident.
Longer recovery time.

🤖 What Evidence-Based RCA Should Do
Instead of producing a generic explanation, a DevOps Copilot should:
Identify the affected workload and container
Collect pod state, events, logs and deployment configuration
Check previous container state and restart history
Correlate the incident with recent changes
Compare desired state with runtime state
Separate symptoms from the actual failure condition
Explain why one cause is more likely than another
Show confidence and missing evidence
Recommend remediation only after diagnosis
👉 Not just “Here is what may have happened.”
👉 “Here is the evidence, reasoning and safest next action.”

🚀 What We’re Building at CrftInfrai
We’re focused on:
👉 Turning Kubernetes incidents into evidence-backed diagnosis and controlled remediation workflows
Across:
Kubernetes health intelligence
Scenario-specific RCA
Pod, event and deployment correlation
Confidence and risk explanations
Policy-aware remediation
Approval-gated GitOps changes
Recovery verification

🌐 Explore CrftInfrai: https://crftinfrai.com
🛠️ Try the Infra Builder: https://lnkd.in/gZif3kJE

🎯 The Shift
From:
👉 “This is probably the root cause.”
To:
👉 “This is the root cause, here is the evidence, and here is the safest recovery path.”

#DevOps #SRE #Kubernetes #AIOps #PlatformEngineering #GCP #AWS #GitOps #Observability  #CloudNative #CrftInfrai

urn:li:activity:7475127521005555712.drafted_comment: I've been working with BEV segmentation lately and something that bugs me about panoptic approaches is the computational overhead during inference. Mask2Former gives you holistic scene understanding, but in real-time LiDAR pipelines, the latency penalty is nontrivial. Have you seen cases where decoupled semantic + instance branches outperform unified panoptic architectures when you're optimizing for fps rather than PQ?
urn:li:activity:7475127521005555712.email_subject: [APPROVE?] Comment on post by AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated) [7475127521005555712]
urn:li:activity:7475127521005555712.author: AI & Machine Learning Community (moderated)
urn:li:activity:7475127521005555712.text:
🧬 𝗦𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝘃𝘀 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝘃𝘀 𝗣𝗮𝗻𝗼𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗦𝗲𝗴𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
𝐴 ℎ𝑖𝑔ℎ-𝑙𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑙 𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒

If you are building computer vision systems; whether for autonomous driving, medical imaging, or robotics, understanding how your model perceives a scene is everything. Here is a quick, high-level translation for your next production pipeline:

1️⃣ 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 Segmentation: "What is in the image?"
● 𝑇ℎ𝑒 𝐺𝑜𝑎𝑙: Classify every single pixel into a category (e.g., road, sky, person).
● 𝑇ℎ𝑒 𝐶𝑎𝑡𝑐ℎ: It treats all instances of a class as a single amorphous blob ("Stuff"). If three people are standing next to each other, they get the exact same color mask.
● 𝐵𝑒𝑠𝑡 𝐹𝑜𝑟: Macro-level environmental understanding, satellite terrain mapping, or medical tissue classification.
● 𝐺𝑜-𝑡𝑜 𝐴𝑟𝑐ℎ𝑖𝑡𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑒: 𝑼-𝑵𝒆𝒕

2️⃣ 𝐈𝐧𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 Segmentation: "Where are the unique objects?"
●𝑇ℎ𝑒 𝐺𝑜𝑎𝑙: Detect and individualize distinct, countable objects ("Things") like cars, pedestrians, or defects.
● 𝑇ℎ𝑒 𝐶𝑎𝑡𝑐ℎ: It completely ignores the background ("Stuff"). The sky, buildings, and roads are left unmapped.
● 𝐵𝑒𝑠𝑡 𝐹𝑜𝑟: Defect counting in manufacturing, inventory tracking, and robotic arms targeting specific items.
● 𝐺𝑜-𝑡𝑜 𝐴𝑟𝑐ℎ𝑖𝑡𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑒: 𝑴𝒂𝒔𝒌 𝑹-𝑪𝑵𝑵

3️⃣ 𝐏𝐚𝐧𝐨𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐜 Segmentation: "The Complete Picture"
● 𝑇ℎ𝑒 𝐺𝑜𝑎𝑙: The ultimate unified approach. It combines Semantic + Instance (Semantic + Instance = Panoptic).
● 𝐻𝑜𝑤 𝑖𝑡 𝑤𝑜𝑟𝑘𝑠: Every single pixel in the image gets labeled. "Stuff" (backgrounds) get a class label, while "Things" (foreground objects) get both a class label and a unique instance ID.
● 𝐵𝑒𝑠𝑡 𝐹𝑜𝑟: Full-scene parsing in autonomous driving and advanced drone navigation where you cannot afford to ignore the road or the pedestrians on it.
● 𝐺𝑜-𝑡𝑜 𝐴𝑟𝑐ℎ𝑖𝑡𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑒: 𝑴𝒂𝒔𝒌2𝑭𝒐𝒓𝒎𝒆𝒓

✦ At-A-Glance Metric Cheat Sheet:
𝗦𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰: Evaluated via mIoU (mean Intersection over Union).
𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲: Evaluated via AP (Average Precision / mAP).
𝗣𝗮𝗻𝗼𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗰: Evaluated via PQ (Panoptic Quality), which mathematically combines Segmentation Quality (SQ) and Recognition Quality (RQ):
PQ = SQ × RQ

urn:li:activity:7475127527187873794.text:
What if I told you that this chaos is actually intelligence being born?

At first glance, it looks like nothing more than thousands of random lines crossing each other.

That’s exactly what I thought when I first started exploring neural networks.

But then I realized something fascinating.

Every connection you see carries information. Every neuron transforms it. Every weight is adjusted through learning. And after millions of tiny mathematical updates, a system that once knew absolutely nothing begins to recognize patterns, solve problems, and make predictions.

The most surprising part isn’t that AI can generate text or images.

It’s that all of it emerges from structures that, on their own, are incredibly simple.

This visualization is a reminder that extraordinary capabilities often arise from countless ordinary interactions working together.

I’m currently building and experimenting with systems like these (project does not belong to me) because understanding what’s happening under the hood is far more exciting than simply using the final product.

If you’re interested in AI, machine learning, and the engineering behind intelligent systems, follow my journey. I share what I build, what I learn, and the ideas that challenge the way we think about technology.
